issue_title: Developer instructions are no longer valid
issue_body:
...at least not with respect to downstream `nupic` project following recent changes there.

Background
------------

From https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core#developer-installation, if you are doing active development on `nupic.core`, and want incremental builds:

```
mkdir -p $NUPIC_CORE/build/scripts
cd $NUPIC_CORE/build/scripts
cmake $NUPIC_CORE -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=../release -DPY_EXTENSIONS_DIR=$NUPIC_CORE/bindings/py/nupic/bindings
```

And then, some variant of `python setup.py develop`.

This is all well and good.  This will result in a `nupic.bindings.egg-link` file in site-packages which points to the python source.  When you import anything from `nupic.bindings`, it comes directly from the source checkout so that changes apply immediately.

Problem
--------

When you proceed to install latest `nupic` at `master`, it will download the `nupic.bindings-0.4.6-py2.7-macosx-10.9-intel.egg` release from pypi and install that directly to site-packages, overriding the version of nupic.bindings under development installed in the steps above.

The reason is nupic specifies `nupic.bindings==0.4.6` in its requirements.txt file, which, at one point, specified 0.4.6.dev0 before it was moved to the root of the project (see https://github.com/numenta/nupic/commit/bbeba9252a14ec25f10a93df8b2e7a9244dd774e).  0.4.6 specified in requirements.txt is older than the current pre-release version (0.4.7.dev0) specified at https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/commit/195cfc8c11f7e2dcb20bd79a46e046f18291b946

I now have the following in site-packages:

```
$ find ~/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages -name "nupic.bindings*"
/Users/amarshall/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/nupic.bindings-0.4.6-py2.7-macosx-10.9-intel.egg
/Users/amarshall/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/nupic.bindings.egg-link
```
And you can see that it loads from the released `0.4.6` version:

```
$ python -c 'import nupic.bindings; print nupic.bindings'
<module 'nupic.bindings' from '/Users/amarshall/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/nupic.bindings-0.4.6-py2.7-macosx-10.9-intel.egg/nupic/bindings/__init__.pyc'>
```
Yet, when I `pip uninstall nupic.bindings`, it removes the link to the newer dev version which is unexpected, and even makes it so I can't remove the older 0.4.6 version, requiring manual intervention (i.e. `rm`ing the .egg file from site-packages):

```
$ pip uninstall nupic.bindings
Uninstalling nupic.bindings-0.4.7.dev0:
  /Users/amarshall/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/nupic.bindings.egg-link
Proceed (y/n)? y
  Successfully uninstalled nupic.bindings-0.4.7.dev0
$ pip uninstall nupic.bindings
Can't uninstall 'nupic.bindings'. No files were found to uninstall.
$ python -c 'import nupic.bindings; print nupic.bindings'
<module 'nupic.bindings' from '/Users/amarshall/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/nupic.bindings-0.4.6-py2.7-macosx-10.9-intel.egg/nupic/bindings/__init__.pyc'>
```

Resolution
----------

I'm not sure what the right workaround is.  I could, technically, `pip uninstall nupic.bindings` and repeat the `nupic.bindings` installation, but that feels wrong.  Perhaps if `nupic` specified `nupic.bindings>=0.4.6` and as a policy always have a value that is greater than, or equal to whatever the latest stable release of `nupic.bindings` is, then you can have the latest `nupic.core` checked out without there being some conflict with `nupic`.  Until then, I don't think there's a clean, obvious, and correct way to maintain a consistent dependency between `nupic` and `nupic.core` for active development.

issue_title: Schema fields don't work with PersistentList and Dict
issue_body:
In https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope.schema/+bug/131112, Damien Genet reported:

> `PersistentList` & `PersistentDict` aren't valid types for `List` & `Dict` schema fields. For instance the following code fails (where `IKLass` is a `zope.schema.List`) :

``` python
class Klass(Peristent):
    list = FieldProperty(IKlass['list'])

    def __init__(self):
        self.list = PersistentList ()
```

Damien attached a patch:  https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope.schema/+bug/131112/+attachment/128401/+files/_field.py.patch

@tseaver followed up:

> The intent of the patch looks reasonable, although it doesn't apply cleanly to the current zope.schema trunk. However, the patch needs to be extended with new tests showing the use of the new `PersistentList` / `PersistentDict` types.

issue_title: FakeResponse Py2 bug, assumes headers can be decoded to unicode using default encoding
issue_body:
On Py2, headers are byte strings, so when [`getOutput` and hence `__str__`](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.app.wsgi/blob/master/src/zope/app/wsgi/testlayer.py#L165) attempts to `encode` them, they must first silently be *decoded* to unicode using the default encoding. If the default encoding is ascii, and the headers  contained any non-ascii values (commonly latin-1), this will fail with a UnicodeError.

This came up in [porting zope.app.form to zope.app.wsgi](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.app.form/pull/1/files#diff-ca01fbf0d85f3dc1bcc9fc0f719502daR76).

issue_title: Py_TPFLAGS_HAVE_CLASS in ExtensionClass.h
issue_body:
The ``PyMem_DEL`` macro in ExtensionClass.h uses `Py_TPFLAGS_HAVE_CLASS` unconditionally on both Python 2 and 3, though it's always true and gone in Python 3. Our `_compat.h` file has a helper macro `HAS_TP_DESCR_GET` for the same check, which should probably be used:

```
#define PyMem_DEL(O)                                   \
  if ((Py_TYPE(O)->tp_flags & Py_TPFLAGS_HAVE_CLASS) \
      && (Py_TYPE(O)->tp_free != NULL))              \
    Py_TYPE(O)->tp_free((PyObject*)(O));             \
  else                                                 \
    PyObject_FREE((O));
```

I'm just not sure if `ExtensionClass.h` should include `_compat.h` now or if there's some problem with that. Or if maybe more of this macro could be avoided under Python 3.

issue_title: Documentation: example using startActivityForResult with bind(on_activity_result=)
issue_body:
I'd like to contribute some documentation. This code opens the Android Photo Gallery and gets the filepath of the selected image.
It took me a few hours to figure out how to do this. The only discussion on it I could find was here:
https://groups.google.com/forum/#!msg/kivy-users/bjsG2j9bptI/-Oe_aGo0newJ

I think it would helpful if an example like this was in the official documentation somewhere. However, I'm not sure if it should be in the p4a or pyjnius docs.
http://python-for-android.readthedocs.org/en/latest/examples/
http://pyjnius.readthedocs.org/en/latest/android.html#accessing-the-activity

I'd be willing to create the documentation and do a pull request, just let me know:
1. Where the documentation should go
2. Any problems with the code below that sets a bad example or other things to change in the example

Just realized, should I activity.unbind at the end of the *on_activity_result* function?

```python
from kivy.logger import Logger
from kivy.clock import Clock

from jnius import autoclass
from jnius import cast

# python-for-android provides this
from android import activity

PythonActivity = autoclass('org.renpy.android.PythonActivity')
Intent = autoclass('android.content.Intent')
Uri = autoclass('android.net.Uri')

# Value of MediaStore.Images.Media.DATA
MediaStore_Images_Media_DATA = "_data"

# Custom request codes
RESULT_LOAD_IMAGE = 1

Activity = autoclass('android.app.Activity')
# Activity is only used to get these codes. Could just hardcode them.
    # /** Standard activity result: operation canceled. */
    # public static final int RESULT_CANCELED    = 0;
    # /** Standard activity result: operation succeeded. */
    # public static final int RESULT_OK           = -1;
    # /** Start of user-defined activity results. */
    # Not sure what this means
    # public static final int RESULT_FIRST_USER   = 1;

def user_select_image(callback):
    """Open Gallery Activity and call callback with absolute image filepath of image user selected.
    None if user canceled.
    """

    # PythonActivity.mActivity is the instance of the current Activity
    # BUT, startActivity is a method from the Activity class, not from our
    # PythonActivity.
    # We need to cast our class into an activity and use it
    currentActivity = cast('android.app.Activity', PythonActivity.mActivity)

    # Forum discussion: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!msg/kivy-users/bjsG2j9bptI/-Oe_aGo0newJ
    def on_activity_result(request_code, result_code, intent):
        if request_code != RESULT_LOAD_IMAGE:
            Logger.warning('user_select_image: ignoring activity result that was not RESULT_LOAD_IMAGE')
            return

        if result_code == Activity.RESULT_CANCELED:
            Clock.schedule_once(lambda dt: callback(None), 0)
            return

        if result_code != Activity.RESULT_OK:
            # This may just go into the void...
            raise NotImplementedError('Unknown result_code "{}"'.format(result_code))

        selectedImage = intent.getData();  # Uri
        filePathColumn = [MediaStore_Images_Media_DATA]; # String[]
        # Cursor
        cursor = currentActivity.getContentResolver().query(selectedImage,
                filePathColumn, None, None, None);
        cursor.moveToFirst();

        # int
        columnIndex = cursor.getColumnIndex(filePathColumn[0]);
        # String
        picturePath = cursor.getString(columnIndex);
        cursor.close();
        Logger.info('android_ui: user_select_image() selected %s', picturePath)

        # This is possibly in a different thread?
        Clock.schedule_once(lambda dt: callback(picturePath), 0)

    # See: http://pyjnius.readthedocs.org/en/latest/android.html
    activity.bind(on_activity_result=on_activity_result)

    intent = Intent()

    # http://programmerguru.com/android-tutorial/how-to-pick-image-from-gallery/
    # http://stackoverflow.com/questions/18416122/open-gallery-app-in-android
    intent.setAction(Intent.ACTION_PICK)
    # TODO internal vs external?
    intent.setData(Uri.parse('content://media/internal/images/media'))
    # TODO setType(Image)?

    currentActivity.startActivityForResult(intent, RESULT_LOAD_IMAGE)
```

issue_title: Startup lines to split with semicolon instead of comma
issue_body:
## Issue
Currently I can't use a IPyhton startup lines like these in Sypder since the line is parsed with comma separation:

```python
from modual import func1, func2
```
or 
```python
import os, sys
```

These common python import structures don't work in startup lines. I actually thought it was broken until I read the note in the ipython config and thought about the implications of using comma separation.

## Solution

If we use semicolon to separate the lines like you can do in python then any valid python should be possible.

Then you could use:
```python
import os, sys; from modal import func1, func2
```

The fix could be as simple as changing this comma to a semicolon in this code, or the line could be executed directly since it is valid python.
 
https://github.com/spyder-ide/spyder-kernels/blob/9a49376ec01ad41af5d3522758e5b4bb30ce85a8/spyder_kernels/console/start.py#L102-L106

## Potential Problem

This is a compatibility concern since the startup lines from 3 would not work in 4, but it's a good time to make a braking change or we can tell that a config file is from 3 it can be upgraded to 4 by replacing the commas with semicolons automatically.

issue_title: `signals.TestAbortClass` does not work in `setup_test` and `on_xxxx` methods
issue_body:
It is valid to abort the class at any stage of a test.

E.g. If we find out in `on_fail` that a test failed due to a fatal error like snippet_client losing socket connection, it is reasonable for the test to abort all subsequent tests in the class instead of continue running and failing more tests when we know they will fail.

issue_title: Please publish Sparkling Water POM files with Spark project dependencies in the "provided", not "compile", scope
issue_body: The Spark dependencies for Sparkling Water as of this writing are defined in the "compile" scope. For example, see http://search.maven.org/remotecontent?filepath=ai/h2o/sparkling-water-core_2.11/2.2.8/sparkling-water-core_2.11-2.2.8.pom. Spark apps run in a container that provides the API and implementation at runtime. Declaring Spark as a "compile" scope dependency can lead to the introduction of conflicting or duplicate classfiles at runtime. This is especially true when building "assembly" jars or "fat jars" which include compile dependencies for deployment, however this is also a problem when using a classloader that can load maven library dependencies at runtime (e.g. Ammonite). See https://maven.apache.org/guides/introduction/introduction-to-dependency-mechanism.html#Dependency_Scope and http://ammonite.io/#import$ivy.

issue_title: Can not fully make use of organization namespace
issue_body:
Consider the following script:

```
cli_flag="created in organization namespace tower-cli script"

# Organization
tower-cli organization create --name="org1" --description="$cli_flag"
tower-cli organization create --name="org2" --description="$cli_flag"

# Inventories
tower-cli inventory create --name="namespace" --description="$cli_flag" --variables="ansible_connection: local" --organization="org1" --description="$cli_flag"
tower-cli host create --name="127.0.0.1" --inventory="namespace"

tower-cli inventory create --name="namespace" --description="$cli_flag" --variables="ansible_connection: local" --organization="org2" --description="$cli_flag"
tower-cli host create --name="127.0.0.1" --inventory="namespace" --organization="org2"
```

The final create command won't work obviously, because `--organization` isn't a valid option for host creation. However, if you do not add this, you get the Multiple Objects Returned error.

For starters here, we should review and update the `identity` tuple for all of the resources in tower-cli.

Bigger picture - tower-cli should support some way to manage inventories, hosts, and other things that share the same name but exist within different organizations / inventories etc that respects the uniqueness rules in Tower itself.

issue_title: default value for EDITOR env variable?
issue_body:
Hi, 

While playing with patronictl options and perusing `invoke_editor()` in `patroni/ctl.py`, I noticed that [`editor_cmd`](https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/32b0768631fcd1f8a75b69627e225a19bece4923/patroni/ctl.py#L954) variable doesn't have a default value.

issue_title: Set check_update to False by default
issue_body:
Currently the value is set to True by default meaning it will issue an additional HTTPS request each time the client is initiated, and polutes the output with the following misleading comment:

    You are running an outdated version of JIRA Python %s. Current version is %s. Do not file any bugs against older versions.

Misleading because the version is not outdated, it is the version I've tested my software against and the users MUST NOT upgrade it as long as they want support for my software.

Pinpointing requirements to a specific version using requirements.txt is one of the common ways to deploy python applications. IMHO the version check is contra productive because of this and I've never seen a library which is doing such a thing.

Please set it to `False` by default or remove it completely.

Instead of the install adding s4cmd.py and leaving it at that, may I suggest adding a file `/usr/local/bin/s4cmd`:

```
#!/usr/bin/python

# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import re

issue_title: Add option to extract lambda env vars to cloudformation parameters
issue_body:
When you run `chalice package`, it would be really helpful if we could take the lambda environment variables you have in your config file and map them to template parameters.  That way you can override these values when you deploy but still reuse the same SAM template.  They'd still default to the existing values in the `.chalice/configjson`.  I'd prefer this was an additional flag you can specify.

The only thing I've ran into is that we'd need to transform the env vars to something supported by cloudformation.  The typical format of `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` for env vars won't work for parameter key names.  We could just camel case them so you'd get `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` -> `UpperSnakeCase`.

issue_title: Entity should not require a Key
issue_body:
Relates to
https://docs.google.com/document/d/1xKw8Tz6lfN5uOcv0my4uUgFSeDoRzwCLho2UGlBpqDU/edit

From the [spec][1]:

> An entity must have a key, unless otherwise documented (for example, an entity in
> <code>Value.entityValue</code> may have no key). An entity's kind is its key's path's 
> last element's kind, or null if it has no key.

Though not required, we should be opinionated (in the constructor) about when it's not necessary if possible.

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-datastore/blob/21e029b2657ebb15fef5429c90c4315326855fd1/proto/datastore_v1.proto

class Ball():

issue_title: PEXBuilder does not respect interpreter when compiling source to bytecode
issue_body:
Today when calling `add_source` on a `PEXBuilder`, any '.py' files are byte code compiled using the current ambient interpreter and not the `PythonInterpreter` the `PEXBuilder` was constructed with: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/v1.0.0/pex/pex_builder.py#L136

This leads to problems when the `PEXBuilder` is being used in an interpeter that has language incompatibilites with the target interpreter; ie: when running under 2.7 and trying to contruct a pex targeted to 3.4 using python 3 only language features.  An example of this is outlined in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/1658

issue_title: PythonInterpreterCache is not robust to `--python-setup-interpeter-constraints` changes.
issue_body:
`PythonInterpreterCache` is a 2-phase object:
1. setup
2. select_interpreter_for_targets

The 1st phase ensures interpreters matching the global `PythonSetup.interpeter_constraints` have been setup once.

The second phase uses interpreters cached in the 1st phase.

Say we run step 1 with interpreter_constraints: `['CPython>=2.7,<3', 'CPython>=3.3']`, we might get cached interpreters for `CPython-2.7.12`, `CPython-2.7.13`, `CPython-2.7.14`, `CPython-3.3.6` and `CPython-3.6.3`.

Now say we widen the interpreter constraints in a future run to: `['CPython>=2.7,<3', 'CPython>=2.6,<3', 'CPython>=3.3']`; ie: we now allow `CPython-2.6.x`. Since the set of cached interpeters `{CPython-2.7.12, CPython-2.7.13, CPython-2.7.14, CPython-3.3.6 and CPython-3.6.3}` satisfies all 3 constraints (the `CPython-2.7.x` satisfy `'CPython>=2.6,<3'`), the `PythonInterpreterCache` will short-circuit setup and now any python target requiring `CPython-2.6.x` will fail to find an interpreter in step 2.

This is the actual issue that was hit in CI for #5068.

issue_title: python PEX application can not establish https connection with properly signed SSL certificate
issue_body:
I can use some help from experts as I am still investigating this issue:

- my python application (using [Apache requests library](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest)) connects to https site with server SSL certificate verification enabled
- the server SSL certificate is signed by trusted CA authority
- the certificate of that CA authority is [correcty installed on the client machine](http://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/karmic/man8/update-ca-certificates.8.html)

issue_title: Buildcache tgz artifacts with null contents should be considered an error
issue_body:
We encountered a case where a tar.gz for a buildcache entry had no files, even though its size was 3.6M.

Upon inspection, the file was not gzipped, in fact it was just full of null bytes, which is apparently a valid tar file.

If pants encounters a file like this, it should consider it a bad cache entry and ignore it.

issue_title: Mutliline values for traceback or stdout/stderr are not properly indented
issue_body:
I have observed this issue with `junit-xml==1.8` and Jenkins `2.46.3`.
In case it matters I'm using xUnit plugin to publish Junit results.

#### Steps to reproduce

Create a test case with a multi-line output or traceback, e.g.

```py
stdout = "line1\nline2"
traceback = "line1\nline2"
tc = TestCase(name=name, classname=classname, stdout=stdout)
tc.add_error_info(output=traceback)
```

#### Expected behavior

In the test case details displayed in Jenkins the multi-line values are shown like

```
line1
line2
```

Based on the formatting of test cases in the xunit plugin, I assume the proper tag to be generated should be: 

```
			<system-out>
				line1
                                line2
			</system-out>
``` 

#### Actual behavior

The first line has an extra indentation:

```
            line1
line2
```

That is caused by the indentation given to text of the relevant tag:

```
			<system-out>
				line1
line2
			</system-out>
```

issue_title: SAM referenceInsertion should record what reads cause reference insertions
issue_body: The code currently records all reference insertions but doesn't keep track of which reads caused insertions. That makes it impossible to ignore insertions according to which read created it.

issue_title: Add Reads filtering to extract a random subset
issue_body: Use the elegant algorithm from Knuth. This needs to be done in a slightly weird way as a `Reads` instance doesn't always know how many reads it has. E.g., a `FastaReads` instance doesn't know its length until all the FASTA has been read.

issue_title: Cycles in build dependencies.
issue_body:
Hi Michael. If you clone https://github.com/mingwandroid/conda/conda-recipes and look in cyclic-test, you'll see a very simplified version of how the R suite of packages are setup (in particular, r itself, r-base the r-recommended meta-package and the r-recommended sub-packages). I renamed r to rust to avoid any conflicts too.

There are a few obvious cycles here. r-recommended loops back immediately to r (for no reason I can see, I expect this can be dropped without any issue at all), then each of the r-recommended sub-packages have a run-dependency back to r also. Some of the r-recommended sub-packages depend on each other and this causes conda-build to enter an infinite loop when it tries to install the earlier-built subpackage (in this case, building rust-pkg2 fails trying to install rust-pkg1).

The old solver presumably detected that it was hitting the exact same situation here and making no progress and well, I'm not sure, but somehow it worked.

To test, this should get you spinning:

    conda-build rust-base rust-recommended
    conda-build rust-pkg1 rust-pkg2 rust-pkg3 --no-test

What do you recommend here?

issue_title: Channels not respected when channel_priority is false
issue_body:
When attempting to reinstall packages from the defaults channel I removed conda-forge from my .condarc channel list but conda continued to install packages from conda-forge.

Perhaps it chooses to update packages from the existing channel they have been installed from? If so I'd consider that to be a bug if the existing channel is no longer in the allowed channel list.

If you try to install gcc with conda on a system which is not having gcc it quits the installation with the following error:

Couldn't locate crtXXX.o in default library search paths. You may not have it  at all. It is usually packaged in libc6-dev/glibc-devel packages. We will try  to locate crtXXX.o with system installed gcc...

3. Only solution so far is to install gcc with apt which needs root rights. And if I can install it with apt I really do not need gcc from conda.

issue_title: With conda being a shell function in 4.4+, what's the suggested way to use conda via scripts when an environment isn't explicitly activated by the user beforehand?
issue_body:
From gitter starting https://gitter.im/conda/conda?at=5ac4e56dc574b1aa3e67a0d8

@natefoo:

> With conda being a shell function in 4.4+, what's the suggested way to use conda via scripts when an environment isn't explicitly activated by the user beforehand?

@mbargull:

> You'd have to explicitly activate the environment beforehand for this to work. I.e., run
>
>     . /path/to/etc/profile.d/conda.sh
>     conda activate base
> If you run that outside of your script, then this would put the conda executable on PATH. If you run it inside of the script, then the script can use the shell function. There are some features, e.g., automatic environment reactivation, that the shell function provides. So if you install/remove packages with (de)activation script into the currently activated environment, you'd want to use the shell function, i.e., source conda.sh inside your script.

@natefoo:

> The issue here is that it's potentially really confusing for users. If I'm delivering a script that is supposed to create an env and install all our dependencies in to it, until 4.4 they didn't need to do anything other than the required conda set up (putting the base env's `bin` on `$PATH`). Now they have to activate the base env before they run the script.
> I can't do it in the script because I don't know where conda is installed if they don't activate the base env. But that wasn't the case before.
> If nothing else, an exported env var for the path to conda.sh would be sufficient.
> Because the function stuff is pretty transparent to users, so it's not going to be readily apparent why they can run conda on the command line but if they call my script in the same shell, it can't run conda.

@mbargull:

> For reference `CONDA_EXE` was introduced in conda/conda#6923.

@natefoo:

> `$CONDA_EXE` only gets you part of the way there, too, since you need the shell functions for `activate` to work, and if you don't have the path to `conda.sh`, you don't have the shell functions. I can use `$(dirname $CONDA_EXE)/../etc/profile.d/conda.sh` to get the path, but that's pretty hacky.
> 
> I guess source `$(dirname $CONDA_EXE)/activate <env>` would also work as long as `activate` and `deactivate` stick around, but it's a hack too.
> 
> The shell check in activate explicitly precludes `. $(dirname $CONDA_EXE)/activate ...` from working under dash. =(

issue_title: Document new_index for use in rollover action
issue_body:
Hello, I'm using curator 5.5 and ES 6.2 and am trying to get the index name in the creation during the rollover step. I can go it manually but can't find an option to do this in curator. Is there a way to do this currently, or can I use this bug as a feature request?

Manual API Call
`POST /filebeat/_rollover/%3Cfilebeat-%7Bnow%2Fm%7BYYYY.MM.dd.HH.mm%7D%7D-1%3E
{
  "conditions": {
    "max_age":   "1d",
    "max_size": "1mb"
  },
  "settings": {
    "index.number_of_shards": 1
  }
}`

Curator Call
`actions:
  1:
    action: rollover
    description: >-
      Rollover the filebeat index
    options:
      name: filebeat
      conditions:
        max_age: 1d
        max_size: 1mb`

Please enter a choice [0]:

issue_title: Issues with 'az network dns record-set create'
issue_body:
Issues with 'az network dns record-set create':
- [x] 1. No description in command (and no description in 'az network dns record-set -h').  Suggest "Create an empty record set in a DNS zone." (PR #1129)
- [x] 2. --zone-name is a mandatory parameter.  People will need to type this a lot.  It needs a short format, I suggest '-z' (PR #1129)
- [x] 3. --type: similarly it would be good to support this as --type -t (PR #1129)
- [x] 4. For --type, you cannot create a SOA record (they're created for you with the zone).  The SOA value is a valid entry in the record type enum, but not supported in this context.  The Help text should reflect this. (PR #1129)
- [x] 5. No support for metadata.  This is a bit like tags on zones (name-value pairs).  Needs to be supported. (PR #1129)
- [x] 6. No support for concurrency.  As with zone create, should support defensive scripting against concurrent operations, e.g. via if-none-match \* to prevent overwriting existing record sets. (PR #1129)

issue_title: Create resource group if not found
issue_body:
Contexts:
1. Almost every create command needs to provide a resource group and a few people have expressed the desire that CLI should create it if not existing 
2. But to create a group, we need `location` which is an optional argument

Propose:
1. We can let CLI handle it at base layer, and expose a configuration point like `auto-create-group`. A user can set it to `true`
2. For the `location` parameter needed to create the group, we can handle it:
    a. If the `location` is supplied at command, use it
    b. Again, we can expose a configuration point of `default location` and CLI will use that.

/cc: @johanste, @colemickens , @squillace

issue_title: Default access log format tries to include Referer header but spells it correctly and thus it doesn't work
issue_body:
The default access log format attempts to output the Referer HTTP request header, referring to it as "Referrer". The latter is the correct spelling of the _word_, but the inventors of the _header_ misspelled it as "Referer". To make this work as intended, the LOG_FORMAT and the logging docs (which show the default log format) should be updated to use the misspelled version of the word. 

```LOG_FORMAT = '%a %t "%r" %s %b "%{Referrer}i" "%{User-Agent}i"'```
vs
```LOG_FORMAT = '%a %t "%r" %s %b "%{Referer}i" "%{User-Agent}i"'```

The docs for the default log format also do not match the actual default log format in that it shows %l and %u which don't actually exist and %t which doesn't appear to be in the default log format.

issue_title: Rationale behind PR series
issue_body:
As per the [docs](https://openupgradelib.readthedocs.io/en/latest/contributing.html#submit-feedback), I want to sum up the rationale behind my recent seris of PR, who mainly are still WIP.

The series comprises:
#40 with it's sister #43 (concerning `get_legacy_columns()`)
#38 (concerning wrapping of `Env`)
#37 (concerning backward-compatibly shelling out map_value_orm to make it able to map m2m)
#36 (small clean up helper for minor migrations, where no full blown migration is warranted)
#35 (concerning xml mapping in map_value as a general improvement)
#45 (concerning the ability of reliably copying m2m fields through the ORM) 

Let's start with the ultimate use case:

As a user of openupgradelib (not OpenUpgrade!), I want to use openupgradelib to help me standardize and optimize the following task:

**Goal:**
I want to update `account.tags` on `account.account` (m2m) based on detecting weather tags of `account.template` have been altered through an updated xml file during migration. (You might question, why I want to do that, but let's stay on topic, I have my reasons.)

**Explanation:**
In order to be able to detect changes in the m2m field I need to know during post migration, what where the previous values. Therefore copying the m2m field preserving all it's relations is achieved by #45 . Clearly, I could manually manipulate the rel_table, but then again, I only can construct a little generic SQL script only for this specific use case and would need to throw it away afterwards. Additionally, manipulating rel_tables is not always intuitive, so this wrapper let's me focus on actual business logic while making future migrations scripts instead of getting stuck by the low level details of SQL queries of mid range complexity to the unexperienced SQLer. Also this task's would get complicated as it is not legit to assume that the target model of a m2m migration remains the same. So forcibly, one would need to keep old column "as is". But, Without salvaging the needed column through copying, it would get lost during module loading. On the simple DB level it's easy, but on m2m it's slightly more complicated. That is why I built #45.

**Details:**
Now as the ORM does already quite a good job in housekeeping tracked fields and columns, despite in the case of m2m (alleged reason for #36), in order for ORM copies to be persisted across module updates, we need to mark them as "manual", which in some ORM routines is indicated by the `manual` flag in `ir_model_fields` yet in others simply by the `x_` prefix. Hence essence of #40 . As often times, constructing mapping dicts by database id's is not something where regex can really be helpful, contrary to xml_ids, #35 tries to solve this. Imagine the need to construct a 2000 lines mapping table based on db id's, I think it is self evident, why this can be quite a headache.
As both, mapping m2m and copying them can only be (afaik) conveniently done, dividing functions into a `_orm` style and a `_sql` style became a natural conclusion. This is not directly related to the Rational of this issue, but includes several benefits, among which would be mentioned a clear separation throughout openupgradelib, when orm mechanisms are  used,or when the lower level pendant is necessary, for example because the ORM is being purposely fooled by the migration script or we need to handle stale columns/tables/values. Having both options side by side permits opting for the best of both worlds and generally increases the usefulness and generality of opernupgradelib. This being said, #38 tries to set the path and establish a common interface for future ORM style methods or refactoring of old ones.

**Backward Compatibility:**
All this has been done with utmost care for backwards compatibility, so that exposed behaviour and known interface would not change, properly emitting deprecation-warnings at the appropriate points, while at the same time permitting the user taking advantage of the new subtleties, in case he needs to.

**Documentation:**
I can agree, that all this needs proper documentation, which has been included into the docstrings, where applicable. But I'm also motivated to undertake a first step towards improving [this](https://openupgradelib.readthedocs.io/en/latest/readme.html#features).

**Further Implications / Thoughts:**
If you are inclined, you can read in the signature of this work the idea of deprecating methods in favor of others. I'm not aware what's openupgrades stance on deprecation. I understand that compatibility is very important, yet I do not know if it *always* wins against maintainability. In our case for example, the majority are or will be enterprise clients, so the main reason why we would commit to contributing to openupgradelib is in order to manage migration of own modules which we do routinely every week since beginning of this year. There is, no doubt a stance about the focus of openupgradelib (whether it is strictly a third wheel of OpenUpgrade, as it could be interpreted from some comments throughout the discussions. Yet, there has been a reason why lib was shelled out, I guess. And it's for this reason, I'm actually contributing, because it serves me very well in another, slightly different use case. This slightly different interpretation of openupgradelib together with a deprecation approach, could solve the compatibility problem also in another more flexible and dynamic way: by pinning down openupgradelib versions to specific OpenUpgrade Migrations.
This could help clean the codebase and ease maintenance while at the same time attracting more people to a dynamic and well conceived project (which as I stated in the mailing list, to my firm belief is an undervalued treasure).


If consens can be drawn on the general rational, I would be very happy to proceed with rigorous compliance with [PR-Guidlines](https://openupgradelib.readthedocs.io/en/latest/contributing.html#pull-request-guidelines) and get everything nice and clean.

I hope, by this I can comply with this resume with the [relvant recommendations](https://openupgradelib.readthedocs.io/en/latest/contributing.html#submit-feedback) for proposing new features.

Thanks everyone for your time and effort, your collaboration and help, and your benevolent contribution to get this initiative proper.

Best Regards,  David

issue_title: Do not populate required fields with random values
issue_body:
By default, calls to method `EntityCreateMixin.create` and several of its siblings causes all required and empty fields to be given random values.

As an example of this, let's say that you do the following:

```python
from nailgun import entities
entities.Repository(name='foo').create()
```

If you do this, then the "content_type", "url" and "product" fields are filled in, as all three of them are marked as required. The "product" field is a foreign key field, so the above code creates a tree of bogus entities. This is great for API testing, but not so much for other users.

Closing this bug requires that the following be done:

* Turn off automatic attribute creation by default.
* Provide some mechanism for enabling automatic attribute creation if so desired by a user.

issue_title: CLI directory options should take absolute path
issue_body:
I was attempting to determine if #443, was still a problem.

I ran letsencrypt --work-dir ../Downloads -vvvv run

I was left with the following error...
```
AH00526: Syntax error on line 10 of /etc/apache2/le_dvsni_cert_challenge.conf:
SSLCertificateFile: file '/etc/Downloads/OwYVJOOHVxWaHWNpNUgNDy4TIafggji3RdP0SymKozo.crt' does not exist or is empty
Action 'configtest' failed.
The Apache error log may have more information. 
```

I thought it to be a peculiar place to be looking for a cert.

The config file is populated with...
```
def get_cert_path(self, achall):
        """Returns standardized name for challenge certificate.
        :param achall: Annotated DVSNI challenge.
        :type achall: :class:`letsencrypt.achallenges.DVSNI`
        :returns: certificate file name
        :rtype: str
        """
        return os.path.join(self.configurator.config.work_dir,
                            achall.chall.encode("token") + ".crt")
```

Thus, leading me to the conclusion that we do not handle our directories appropriately from the command-line.  I think it would be much easier if all of the work_dir, config_dir, and logs_dir were guaranteed to be an absolute path.

Providing the full path off the command-line did solve the problem.

Can we find a place to normalize all paths?

issue_title: Option to avoid computing total count
issue_body:
Right now, the `Paginator` class computes a `total_count` of all objects to return as part of the `"meta"` portion of the api response.  In most casees, I think that seems pretty reasonable.  However, for very large databases (I have a use case in mind with tens or hundreds of millions of rows), the act of simply computing an accurate count can be very slow.  This is already acknowledged in the docs, where there is a very thoughtfully written implementation of a reasonable "approximate count" solution with Postgres in mind (not sure how well it works for other backends).

With that in mind, would it make sense to add a configurable option to avoid computing totals in the `"meta"` section of the api response?  I.e. if you chose `compute_totals=False` then the API simply wouldn't compute totals at all.  But the default could be `compute_totals=True`.

issue_title: Soundness/Performance Bug with fp.sqrt
issue_body:
On the `unstable` branch, at commit d4b66538f93a4e9b255690bf127ffae3f4cc493c, consider the following input:

```
(set-logic QF_FP)
(declare-fun x0 () (_ FloatingPoint 3 4))
(check-sat)
(assert (= (fp.sqrt RNE x0) (fp.sqrt RNE (fp #b0 #b000 #b001))))
(check-sat)
```

Z3 incorrectly reports that this is `unsat`.  In this case, it's not necessary to reason about what `fp.sqrt` does in order to see that this is incorrect; if it is treated as an uninterpreted function, then a satisfying result would be `x0 =  (fp #b0 #b000 #b001)`.

The `unsat` result is specific to the small number of bits used.  If things are expanded to be 32 bits, then Z3 reports `sat`.  Such an expanded version is shown below:

```
(set-option :produce-models true)
(set-logic QF_FP)
(declare-fun x0 () Float32)
(check-sat)
(assert (= (fp.sqrt RNE x0) (fp.sqrt RNE (fp #b0 #b00000000 #b00000000000000000000001))))
(check-sat)
(get-model)
```

However, I noticed in this situation that Z3 hangs relative to MathSAT5, with MathSAT5 taking under 100ms, whereas Z3 takes nearly 9 seconds.  I'm not sure if this is related to the same underlying issue or not, but I figured it's worth reporting.

issue_title: Z3 4.7.1 segfault during Augur contracts analysis
issue_body:
Oyente, Ethereum smart contracts analysis tool, which runs Z3 solver under the hood fails with a Z3 segfault when Z3 solver is upgraded to 4.7.1 and Oyente tool is analysing Augur smart contracts.

The segfault does not happen on Z3 4.5.0 and 4.6.0.

Steps to reproduce:

Those commands run Oyente with 4.5.0 solver:

```
 $  git clone https://github.com/cryptomental/augur-core.git
 $  cd augur-core/
 $  git co feature/ignore-selected-oyente-issues
 $  npm run docker:run:test:security:oyente
```

Switching to 4.7.1 can be done with the following patch in source/support/test/oyente/Dockerfile :

```
diff --git a/source/support/test/oyente/Dockerfile b/source/support/test/oyente/Dockerfile
index e12061d5..43acfb23 100644
--- a/source/support/test/oyente/Dockerfile
+++ b/source/support/test/oyente/Dockerfile
@@ -11,9 +11,9 @@ RUN apt-get update \
        && apt-get install -y software-properties-common \
        && apt-get install -y unzip
 
-RUN wget --quiet https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/archive/z3-4.5.0.zip \
-       && unzip z3-4.5.0.zip -d /tmp \
-       && cd /tmp/z3-z3-4.5.0 \
+RUN wget --quiet https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/archive/z3-4.7.1.zip \
+       && unzip z3-4.7.1.zip -d /tmp \
+       && cd /tmp/z3-z3-4.7.1 \
        && python scripts/mk_make.py --python \
        && cd build \
        && make -j8 \
```

Full Oyente log with segfault:

https://gist.github.com/cryptomental/c5ff857becfeda48c8c0fbb432eeba90

Verbose Oyente log including ASM dump:

https://gist.github.com/cryptomental/04e70f3b849573980814a29aa45134ac

References:

https://github.com/AugurProject/augur-core/issues/689
https://github.com/melonproject/oyente/issues/339

issue_title: remove manual hoisting when updating to the next lerna release
issue_body:
in #1420, we worked around a known bug in lerna 2.0.0-beta.36 by manually removing the `immutable` library from `packages/commutable/node_modules/immutable` (because it is problematic for flow.

When we update to the next release of lerna, we should add back the `--hoist immutable` flag to our lerna command in `package.json` and remove the `rimraf /packages.../immutable` portion.

issue_title: Is Resource owner flow possible or not and if yes how?
issue_body:
I'm coming from #581 which involves some knowledge and some questions that are kinda off topic there and therefore I made a new issue.

The problem I have is that the documentation is to centric on using some html views that ship with this plugin. My goal is to create a single page application that authorizes itself in a similar way as any other potential client would do via oauth2. For this I basically want to follow the resource owner flow as described in the rfc here: https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-4.3

So I want to 

1. let user enter credentials in a form
2. make a POST request with those credentials
3. receive the oauth token as a response when the credentials are correct

Is this even possible with django-oauth-toolkit or is dot not meant to be used for this flow? Please explain how if the answer is that its possible. I'm happy to PR an understandable documentation if things are clear then.

issue_title: Allow factorial specification of tests based on sets of options
issue_body:
The pyutilib.testdriver should allow specifying multiple sets of options for a solver. It would then run a "full-factorial" suite of tests. For example:

```testsuites:
  solvers:
    solver_a:
      np:
        - 1
        - 2
        - 4
      strategy:
        - dive
        - pseudocost
    solver_b:
      np: 1
  problems:
    problem_1:
    problem_2:
```

would specify 14 tests.

This is on the wishlist because this capability has lots of difficult implications, including how it gets implemented, how fractional specifications are managed (in the 'tests' block), and how tests get named. If this was spelled out in more detail, I'd be willing to look into this. However, this might be a nice extension for John or Dave to dig into to help familiarize themselves with this code.

It is an open question how to consistently name the individual tests.

issue_title: Gateway management threads not cleaned up
issue_body:
https://github.com/ibm-watson-iot/iot-python/blob/dd0451238c49c297e7c9a3b2c0f23fe5bcaa29d9/src/ibmiotf/gateway.py#L544

A new thread is started when a manage event is received (via `threading.Timer`), which is never joined/cancelled. This in turn starts a new thread without cancelling/joining the old thread, etc. This means that after running for a few days we're getting OS errors because our process has hundreds of threads and can't create any more.

issue_title: Upscore implementation may not perform as intended.
issue_body:
It appears as the upscore layer is intended to upscore a feature map.  Similiar to how the Deconv and Bilinear keral works in Caffe.

http://caffe.berkeleyvision.org/doxygen/classcaffe_1_1BilinearFiller.html

Their implementation mentions that the usage of Deconv (i.e. conv2d_transpose + bilinear kernel) should be the same as performing the following on an input image

```
import skimage.transform
plt.imshow(skimage.transform.rescale(image[:,:,0], 2, mode='constant', cval=0))
```

If the upscore implementation is intended to behave in a similar way, it does not.  Passing an image through this layer to upsample to twice the size results in noise.

The reason for this is that for an input with C_i channels, output with C_o channels, and stride of S, the layer creates a kernel of size [S,S,C_i,C_o]

This successfully works with tensorflows conv2d_transpose function, however as a result the kernal does not bi-linear filter each channel of the input to create an output, instead it sums over all channels of the input to create one of the output channels.   

In order to make this layer work properly, the input object should be unpacked along the channels dimension.  Each channel should be conv2d_transposed with a kernel of size [S,S,1, 1] and then the resulting maps should be packed back into an output of size [B H W C_o]

** Note **

This is not a very elegant solution due to the unpacking and packing, but it is unclear how to get this functionality out of the tf.conv2d_transpose function.

issue_title: SMOTE With Non-Continuous Data
issue_body:
I have a dataset with a feature that takes on only integer values (a performance rating, for example).  Since SMOTE generates data along a continuous vector between two points, if they had differing values of this feature, the resulting point has a non-integer value.  Using a decision tree, it quickly learns that these non-integer values are a good predictor of being in the minority class.  For example, I end up with tree splits like:  if performance <4 and then the next split is if performance > 4 to a leaf with all minority class data (all generated by SMOTE).

For example, a leaf of all SMOTE data when Performance Rating is 3 < x < 4.
![smote-nc](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/43162/18696663/ea0dd88a-7f88-11e6-8956-9a5310d9afbd.png)


In the original SMOTE paper, the authors suggested a SMOTE-NC for non-continuous data that would use the median of k-nearest neighbors for those non-continuous features.  Is there is will to implement this feature?  I suppose the user would need to pass the an index list of the non-continuous features over what is currently being passed to the algorithm.

issue_title: Support for backend email template variable sending.
issue_body:
Mandrill uses a concept called `merge_vars`, SendGrid template variables are called `substitutions`, and Postmark has a `TemplateModel` concept.

While all three services receive template variables differently within their API, they have a common philosophy on setting programmatic key value pairs to leverage on their respective services.

What does that look like via the EmailBackend framework, I am not sure at the moment. However, I think this feature request can fall within the scope of this library.

issue_title: Model layout broken with Django 2.1
issue_body:
Django 2.1 prevents models from having more than one primary key. It seems it only checks this in the system check:

```
examples.LdapGroup: (models.E026) The model cannot have more than one field with 'primary_key=True'.
```

This breaks not only the composite RDN feature but django-ldapdb as a whole, because django-ldapdb always has two primary keys on the model (dn and the selected RDN field).

issue_title: Webroot Plugin: Cleanup Error
issue_body:
Hi! I'm using the letsencrypt client from a docker container with the following command:
`letsencrypt-auto certonly --agree-tos -m email@domain.com -d "domain.com" --webroot --webroot-path=/srv/www/letsencrypt`

The command fails with this error: 
`An unexpected error occurred:
OSError: [Errno 16] Device or resource busy: '/srv/www/letsencrypt/.well-known/acme-challenge'
Please see the logfiles in /var/log/letsencrypt for more details.`

The log file shows the exception that has been thrown here:
`2016-05-01 06:02:09,853:DEBUG:letsencrypt.main:Exiting abnormally:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/bin/letsencrypt", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/main.py", line 692, in main
    return config.func(config, plugins)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/main.py", line 509, in obtain_cert
    _, action = _auth_from_domains(le_client, config, domains, lineage)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/main.py", line 93, in _auth_from_domains
    lineage = le_client.obtain_and_enroll_certificate(domains)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/client.py", line 274, in obtain_and_enroll_certificate
    certr, chain, key, _ = self.obtain_certificate(domains)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/client.py", line 246, in obtain_certificate
    self.config.allow_subset_of_names)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/auth_handler.py", line 74, in get_authorizations
    self._respond(resp, best_effort)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/auth_handler.py", line 134, in _respond
    self._cleanup_challenges(active_achalls)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/auth_handler.py", line 266, in _cleanup_challenges
    self.auth.cleanup(achalls)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/plugins/webroot.py", line 234, in cleanup
    os.rmdir(root_path)
OSError: [Errno 16] Device or resource busy: '/srv/www/letsencrypt/.well-known/acme-challenge'`

Apparently it's related to the cleanup process. The interesting thing is that the text-file used for authorization actually is being deleted although the error implies otherwise. I commented out the following line:
https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/certbot/plugins/webroot.py#L234
Afterwards, everything worked as expected and even the text-file for authorization had been deleted (although that sounds strange to me). It looks like the removal of this file is being triggered multiple times.

I hope that helps you to narrow the problem down and fix it for further releases.

issue_title: When writing a NDEF tag, the WriteFlag is not set correctly (i.e. indicates 'Write in Progress' even when write was successful)
issue_body: In tt3.py the variable for WriteFlag is set to 0x0F instead of 0x00 at the end.

issue_title: Kafka restart can be improved
issue_body:
Currently kafka restart is not very safe IMHO. With buku we had a situation that kafka did a start before the old one finished shutdown. We also do what buku does:
process.terminate()
process.wait()
So that doesn't guarantee that old kafka stopped. 
I know that here we additionally check that node doesn't exist in ZK any more.
But I think to be on the safe side we can also check that Java process has ended (in a hacky way)

issue_title: Create "Echo Node" for the test network
issue_body:
We want to provide one or more "echo nodes" -- raiden addresses that respond to transfers in a predictable way. The goal is to allow a user to test that raiden works (tbd) without requiring the user to run more than one node themselves.

### Mode of operation:

- for transfers with an amount that satisfies `amount % 3 == 0`, it sends a transfer with an amount of `amount - 1` back to the initiator
- for transfers with a "lucky number" amount `amount == 7` it does not send anything back immediately -- after having received "lucky number transfers" from 7 different addresses it sends a transfer with `amount = 49` to one randomly chosen one (from the 7 lucky addresses)
- for all other transfers it sends a transfer with the same `amount` back to the initiator

### Tasklist:
- [ ] create an echo node runner derived from `app.py`
- [ ] hook into `EventTransferReceivedSuccess` and initiate the response
- [ ] log the received transfers and the special events

issue_title: Warn users that POST requests sent over non-HTTPS may fail
issue_body:
In some cases, Canvas's API responds unpredictably when sending POST requests over HTTP instead of HTTPS.

For example, the `account.create_course` call will return the `GET` response if made over HTTP instead of HTTPS. This breaks `CanvasObject`'s attempts to automatically create an object:

```
>>> a.create_course(course={'name': 'JSA_TST1337_Jesse_McBride'})
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<console>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/canvasapi/account.py", line 77, in create_course
    return Course(self._requester, response.json())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/canvasapi/canvas_object.py", line 28, in __init__
    self.set_attributes(attributes)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/canvasapi/canvas_object.py", line 68, in set_attributes
    for attribute, value in attributes.items():
AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'items'
```

It seems wrong to stop users at every POST call if they've set their `CANVAS_URL` to `http://*`. Instead, I think we should hook into `Requester._post_request` and raise a warning that the API call may not succeed.

issue_title: Reliably disable keyboard events
issue_body:
I have an interactive ipywidget for which I would like to use keyboard shortcuts. Although it's not ideal, I'd like to have essentially two modes, either "in-my-widget" mode or "not-in-my-widget" (i.e. in Jupyter) mode. For now, let's say my ipywidget has a button that toggles between the two modes.

The Jupyter KeyboardManager has a [disable](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/ca50f1af5afb525fd95b98b2e61e69db28094467/notebook/static/notebook/js/keyboardmanager.js#L234) method that could theoretically be used for this purpose, but the issue is that this is a global, forgetful (i.e. not stack-ful) bit of state. Specifically, in ipywidgets, the widget area is registered with the keyboard manager via [register_events](https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/4aceb8670a64aff89c528d5d00205f1428c68d16/widgetsnbextension/src/extension.js#L144), which means that every time the widget area is focused or blurred, any other attempts to change the KeyboardManager settings [are erased](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/ca50f1af5afb525fd95b98b2e61e69db28094467/notebook/static/notebook/js/keyboardmanager.js#L241-L246). This produces a poor interaction paradigm where if a user accidentally clicks outside the widget area (or generally does any blurring action, which in practice seems to happen more frequently than I expect), then the keyboard shortcuts reactivate.

Is there some way I can more reliably disable the Jupyter keyboard events to preclude this issue?

issue_title: Propose to hard-code lineage data path in the dataset metadata document
issue_body:
In datacube there is this concept of "Product Metadata". It is a mapping from "well known name" to a path within a metadata document containing the value of a variable with a "well known name". It is an indirection mechanism that allows to treat datasets of different type in a consistent fashion. For example `id` is a property that every dataset ought to have, but it can be stored in the metadata document under different name/location depending on the product. This mechanism is particularly useful when metadata document generation is outside of the ODC control, and instead of massaging original metadata into a consistent form ODC expects, one can just define a custom metadata document that describes where information can be found. 

`sources` is the name used to point to a document sub-tree containing lineage data. 

https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/fbefaa5f95704160133cd45df4cd5a12e1ea88f9/datacube/index/default-metadata-types.yaml#L50-L54

This is fine and consistent with the approach of having metadata indirection mechanism, but it comes with some significant limitations. Lineage location being a "dynamic property" like this makes it impossible to traverse lineage tree without first associating every dataset along the lineage path to a product. This is needed since Metadata is a property of a product. 

In the current system where lineage is "All or Nothing" deal (you can either skip all of it, or otherwise all lineage should be indexable), this coupling of traversal to product matching is annoying from implementation perspective but is not a deal breaker. But if we ever want to implement in-between-solutions where some of the lineage data is recorded, but not mapped to actual datasets in the database, this inability to navigate lineage tree without establishing `dataset<->product` mappings for every node in the lineage tree becomes a real problem.

We can of course have partial solutions like "assume eo metadata scheme" for those datasets that do not map to any product in the database. But this feels like "hard-coding by convention", rather than demanding a certain path for lineage data.

issue_title: Store core span properties in their raw forms
issue_body:
(Somewhat related to #105)

I would like to propose that some of the span properties (like `start_time`/`end_time`, `span_id`/`trace_id`) are stored in a more canonical format (eg, raw `datetime.datetime` and `uuid.UUID` respectively) inside the `Span` object. We can then defer the formatting of this value to whichever exporter is being used. 

For example, right now `start_time` undergoes the following transformations when using the ZipkinExporter:
 - When it's [created][1]: `datetime.datetime` -> `str`
 - When it's [exported][2]: `str` -> `datetime.datetime` -> `int`

If we stored it as a raw `datetime.datetime` object, we could simply convert it to an `int` when reporting and subtract. Another possible solution is to store the `start_time` and `end_time` separately from the duration. This could allow us to use something higher res like `time.perf_counter()` (at least in Python3) for calculating the duration.

[1]: https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/cfcfda584ba984e6f0e89684120c1ea94271b2c9/opencensus/trace/span.py#L190
[2]: https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/master/opencensus/trace/exporters/zipkin_exporter.py#L137-L149

issue_title: Changing parameter values in the modeldescription.xml-file is dangerous
issue_body:
In FMPy 0.2.3., writing new parameter values to the xml file has been introduced.
However, this is dangerous, as it is not specified, whether an importing tool should consider these values or the hard coded values, see this discussion: [https://trac.fmi-standard.org/ticket/387].
Strictly speaking, changing the modeldescription.xml could even invalidate the FMU (consistency of xml and binary could be checked by creating a GUID with by a checksum.
The clean way to support changed parameter and even different parameter sets is the coming SSP standard.

The code in `layers.py` around line number 5438.
```
        if dropout:
            self.cell = DropoutWrapper_fn(self.cell, input_keep_prob=1.0, output_keep_prob=out_keep_prob)
```

Why Dropout Wrapper the self.cell (the self.cell is already wrap by dropout) again. I think we should delete this line.

issue_title: Is it possible to include the source code in Tree nodes?
issue_body:
Hello lark developers,

Thanks very much for providing this wonderful tool. I am wondering if it is possible to attach the original source code to each Tree node.

My current use case is to raise error messages for certain Tree node. Even though we can reconstruct the source code by recursively visit a Tree node, it would be much easier if there is a way to access the original source code.

~~~
start: first "," second
first: ...
second: ... (a very complex definition)

===========parse.py===============
def interpret(t):
    if t.data == 'start':
        interpret(t.children[0])
        interpret(t.children[1])
    elif t.data =='first':
        # do something
       ...
    elif t.data =='second':
        print(t.source) <- Is this possible?
~~~

issue_title: Enabling multiple instances of an output (ends up being map reduce)
issue_body: We need to enable multiple instances of an output. This will allow a "streaming batch" semantic in the pipeline that spawns multiple copies of a downstream DAG. There are a lot of challenges to solve here as you would also want a way to coalesce multiple outstanding subdags. This is essentially map-reduce.

issue_title: flatten of mapping produces dot-prefixed keys
issue_body:
```python

import colander
import pprint

class Friend(colander.TupleSchema):
    rank = colander.SchemaNode(colander.Int(),
                               validator=colander.Range(0, 9999))
    name = colander.SchemaNode(colander.String())

class Phone(colander.MappingSchema):
    location = colander.SchemaNode(colander.String(),
                                  validator=colander.OneOf(['home', 'work']))
    number = colander.SchemaNode(colander.String())

class Friends(colander.SequenceSchema):
    friend = Friend()

class Phones(colander.SequenceSchema):
    phone = Phone()

class Person(colander.MappingSchema):
    name = colander.SchemaNode(colander.String())
    age = colander.SchemaNode(colander.Int(),
                             validator=colander.Range(0, 200))
    friends = Friends()
    phones = Phones()

schema = Person()
appstruct = {
  'name':'keith',
  'age':20,
  'friends':[(1, 'jim'),(2, 'bob'), (3, 'joe'), (4, 'fred')],
  'phones':[{'location':'home', 'number':'555-1212'},
            {'location':'work', 'number':'555-8989'},],
  }

pprint.pprint(schema.flatten(appstruct))

# produces:

# {'.age': 20,
#  '.friends.0.name': 'jim',
#  '.friends.0.rank': 1,
#  '.friends.1.name': 'bob',
#  '.friends.1.rank': 2,
#  '.friends.2.name': 'joe',
#  '.friends.2.rank': 3,
#  '.friends.3.name': 'fred',
#  '.friends.3.rank': 4,
#  '.name': 'keith',
#  '.phones.0.location': 'home',
#  '.phones.0.number': '555-1212',
#  '.phones.1.location': 'work',
#  '.phones.1.number': '555-8989'}

# should produce?:

# {'age': 20,
#  'friends.0.name': 'jim',
#  'friends.0.rank': 1,
#  'friends.1.name': 'bob',
#  'friends.1.rank': 2,
#  'friends.2.name': 'joe',
#  'friends.2.rank': 3,
#  'friends.3.name': 'fred',
#  'friends.3.rank': 4,
#  'name': 'keith',
#  'phones.0.location': 'home',
#  'phones.0.number': '555-1212',
#  'phones.1.location': 'work',
#  'phones.1.number': '555-8989'}
```

I think this is the right fix, but it'd be nice to get some confirmation from @chrisrossi:

https://github.com/Pylons/colander/compare/fix.rootflatten
